text_en: @Merlin: well, look at Bellingcat's sources, which is designated in Russia as a media under foreign influence, meaning anti-Putin. To give you an idea, Bellingcat is in the crosshairs because of their investigations into the poisonings of Skripal and Navalny and the destruction of the airliner by the separatists. Amusing: Bellingcat even found former fascists from the French GUD proudly displaying Nazi runes which are the symbol of the Azov battalion. Haaretz is the center-left Israeli newspaper, which is concerned about the organization by Azov and Pravi Sektor of "self-defense courses" within the Ukrainian population and the possibility of a far-right coup against Zelensky. This is indeed the biggest danger currently for Ukraine: the death of Zelensky, whether by the Russians or by radical Ukrainians, which would plunge Ukraine into a civil war like in Yugoslavia. There are also extremists on the separatist side.

text_en: MIRANDOLE: don't be cold in the back. I am Basque, the son of a nationalist-communist, I divorced the communist when I was 14/15 years old, but from my father and mother, I retained a deep awareness of my ethnic belonging. So I am a supporter of the independence of Euskal Herria (the seven provinces). But rest assured, once independent, we Basques have no ambition to subjugate Aragon and extend to Bordeaux, in the night of time, ex-Basque territories (hence the word Gascon). We will be a sort of Netherlands, Singapore, Switzerland, where Euskara (Basque) will be the official language, exclusive to the administration. But French and Castilian will have a status that will ensure their longevity. Every public servant in contact with the public must know one or the other, or ideally both. Being trilingual is accessible to everyone, unless one is a purist. Muddling through a language is better than not speaking it.

text_en: Excellent text like most of MG who is a bit of a troublemaker in this journal full of good thoughts. Yes, there is no simple truth and the story of cowboys and Indians is much more complex than in the 50s. Russian art is immense like this country and is indispensable to us. It is out of the question to ban Russian music for example, and who still plays Khrennikov, supporter of the sinister Zhdanov who banned Shostakovich or Prokofiev for formalism (1948)? Time has done its work. Many performers have declared against the war or even Putin, indeed already well established in their careers. What about the very young instrumentalists burning for their piano and violin and blocked in their careers? Finally, Russian troops are approaching beautiful Odessa where violinists M Elman, D Oistrakh or N Milstein as well as W Horowitz were born! Very sad times for music lovers.

A Dr. Strangelove at our doorstep. What solution is there to this? - Ukraine being unable to win and I haven't really seen a popular uprising like, in its time, in Prague or Budapest, it is likely that a government in Moscow's pay with Stalinist trials will follow. And Bach, what new piece will he come up with to explain to us that sports and the money it brings are above all this!!! Does he really think it will pass? This graft had indeed succeeded quite quickly in Japan, Germany, and Italy, and eventually in South Korea. In 2003, the USA invaded Iraq, one of the bloodiest dictatorships in the world, while in 2022, Putin invades a country that is certainly very corrupt but democratic (free elections and fundamental freedoms respected) under the pretext of "denazifying" a country with a Jewish president! There is one aspect you forget, which is that Russia is the last terrestrial empire from the 19th century and that the legitimacy of its domination over Central and Eastern Asia and the "autonomous republics" remains to be proven. I mainly see an independence of this Central and Eastern Asia duly decolonized, a retraction of Russia to the west of the Ural Mountains, and an adhesion, in the more or less long term and according to its own modalities, to the European project. text_en: I had the same thought about Ukraine's candidacy for the EU. If it does not join NATO, and as long as the EU is not a strategic power, then Ukraine will constantly be a hostage of Russia and an extraordinary lever to paralyze the EU. I also admit that I am not very comfortable regarding Sweden and Finland (and to a lesser extent Austria and Ireland). If Russia starts hitting them, NATO will probably not move any more than for Ukraine, and the EU will have no choice but to lift sanctions to come to the aid of its two member states. We need to do things in the right order: first, let’s make the EU a strategic power (and I really don’t see how that would happen without integrating governance), then let’s see about possible enlargement. I know that the EU has often been built by putting the cart before the horse, but this time, I believe we are reaching the limit.

text_en: (wikipedia): "Cut off from the rest of the country, the capital quickly suffers the exceptional rigor of a winter (peaks at -12 °C in December) while German bombings worsen the situation starting in January 1871. Rationing of goods is organized late, queues lengthen in front of food stores literally besieged. The meat ration, consumed almost daily at that time by Parisians, is organized. The prices of meat, canned goods, bread, and goods soar. Bakers sell a black bread of unknown composition. The bourgeoisie begins to slaughter horses, which the poor had been the only ones to consume until then. They even eat cats, dogs, and rats. The animals of the Jardin des Plantes are slaughtered. The inhabitants are deprived of wood and coal, without gas, the streets are plunged into darkness. Mortality doubles in a few months (pulmonary diseases due to cold and malnutrition)."

Therefore, Ukraine could not plausibly join, which now allows Russia to invade the country... under the pretext that it wanted to join NATO: a snake biting its own tail, an unsolvable geopolitical puzzle. That said, we must not be mistaken about responsibilities. The missiles, tanks, and fighter jets that are killing people today are indeed Russian, period. text_en: We must dispel a recurring illusion regarding the meaning of the term negotiation. From the side of an authoritarian or despotic regime whose main logic is the assertion of force and, if possible, its increase or extension, a negotiation is merely a tool used in a strategy that does not exclude cunning or cynicism. For democracies that respect often opposing values, negotiation, in itself, is already one as it involves the idea of an exchange with the aim of an agreement. Upon reaching an agreement, the two protagonists will not therefore put the same meaning on it but do not necessarily have the same awareness of this difference. text_en: An article unworthy of the decoders. If I understand correctly, an EU country that produces 99% of the gas consumed locally and imports 1% of Russian gas would be counted as 100% dependent on Russian gas. For example, France, which imports less than 17% of its gas from Russia, is considered to be dependent on Russia to the extent of 25 to 50%. For oil, the figures confuse refined crude oil in a country and total oil consumption, while for example in France we import 45Mt of crude but also 30Mt of refined products mainly from the Middle East. In 2019, France imported only 6.2Mt of crude oil from Russia.

text_en: Continuation: Acquisition of Swiss nationality by naturalization or reintegration. Switzerland has three forms of acquiring nationality by decision of the authorities. The procedure differs depending on the personal situation of the foreign national: Ordinary naturalization is open to anyone residing in Switzerland for at least ten years, of which three in the five years preceding the application, and holding a residence permit (permit C). Facilitated naturalization is open in particular to - people married to a Swiss citizen; - foreigners of the third generation who were born in Switzerland. There are other facilitated naturalization procedures, for example for stateless persons. Reintegration is open to people who have lost Swiss nationality due to expiration, release, or loss of Swiss nationality.

text_en: To extend the historical metaphor, I find there is a striking parallel between Doriot and Mélenchon! Both were leaders of established parties (PC for one and PS for the other), both founded their own movement to represent an independent and popular left, especially anti-capitalist (PPF for one and LFI for the other), both were remarkable orators and recognized organizers. Both were fascinated by authoritarian regimes. Both gradually drifted towards extreme positions. There is even a slight physical resemblance between these two men. No one can say what Mélenchon's choices would have been placed in Doriot's historical situation, but I find it interesting to recall this striking parallel!

text_en: When lying, one must do it with a bit more skill. On February 9, 1990, and three times, US Secretary of State James Baker stated before Gorbachev that NATO would not seek to expand eastward. On May 18, Baker added: "Before saying a few words about the German question, I wanted to emphasize that our policies are not aimed at separating Eastern Europe from the Soviet Union." In December 1989, in Malta, George H.W. Bush assured his Soviet counterpart that he "would not take advantage of the revolutions in Eastern Europe to harm the interests of the USSR." On January 31, 1990, in Tutzing, Hans-Dietrich Genscher (German Foreign Minister) stated that "the changes in Eastern Europe and the reunification process should not go against the security interests of the Soviet Union," and urged NATO to "exclude any progression of the Atlantic Alliance eastward." Lies, lies, lies.

text_en: @ GGR. There is only one way to be sovereign, and that is to be it. Sovereign means superior, so either we consider that we have no superior and we are sovereign, or we submit to a superior and we are no longer sovereign. It's as simple as that. At least if words have meaning. Being sovereign does not prevent alliances or temporary partial delegations of sovereignty, etc., but being sovereign is not shared. Formulas like "a sovereign France in a sovereign Europe" make no sense except, in shorthand, this: let’s enjoy the American umbrella, let’s be vassals of the USA. Being sovereign, for what purpose? So that France is free, so that it governs itself. Isn't freedom the first word of our motto? Let’s be free! That would be a revolution, full of risks, it would require enormous courage, it would make France the pariah of the so-called free world. But I have learned that freedom has no price...

text_en: I agree with you, except on the number of deaths related to Chernobyl and Fukushima: Chernobyl, there are 134 people who suffered acute irradiation (the main symptoms being skin lesions + cataracts), of which about thirty died. And an excess of about 6000 thyroid cancers related to the consumption of contaminated milk and meat (Iodine-131 and Cs-137), which resulted in 15 deaths at t+20 years. For the rest, the average doses taken by evacuated populations and Europeans are too low to be statistically detectable to this day. The number of deaths that we sometimes hear about are not observations (no incidence has been observed so far except for the above), they are extrapolations of laws obtained at average doses and high dose rates supposed to be applicable to low doses (but not yet proven). And these are not deaths, but (pessimistic) forecasts of cancers. Fukushima: no (yet) deaths related to the dose.

text_en: A tactical nuclear bomb would have a very marginal impact on military proceedings. We are talking about an effect over a radius of 2 or 3 kilometers while the front line is thousands of kilometers long. There is not enough troop concentration to annihilate the opposing army, not to mention the risk of impacts on one's own troops. As noted below, it would also hit Russian territory itself (from Russia's perspective). This would also trigger a massive conventional response from NATO (according to NATO officials) and would annihilate the already relative support from China. China is too afraid that a nuclear attack would break the deterrence consensus and encourage Taiwan to arm itself with the bomb. That said, there are many other steps in the escalation before reaching the tactical nuclear bomb. Putin has many other filth in reserve.

text_en: It took 10 years with Iran... When these sanctions were applied at the beginning of the conflict, the idea was to bring the Russian economy to its knees in a few months. The goal was not achieved because the Russian economy is known to be resilient and that the majority of countries in the world refused to apply them, including Turkey, a NATO member. The Russian response hurt more than expected and is likely to hurt even more in the coming months. Judging that the sanctions have not yielded the expected results is not being pro-Putin; it is simply a fact. And then other actions like training Ukrainian soldiers, providing specific weapons, coordinating with American intelligence, have yielded much more impressive results than expected. Why cling to saying that something that doesn't work, works? A mystery.

text_en: France lost the war and its independence in 1940. The United Kingdom won this war but lost its independence and its empire. It was in the middle of the 20th century that the center of the world shifted to the United States. Just like the defeated and demilitarized Germany and Japan, France has since then been its ally. It did try to break free from this submission, up to Chirac who refused to be taken into its expeditionary forces in the Middle East. This vassal status cannot be called into question due to its economic alliance with Germany and its membership in the European Union. The great enemy of the Americans was and remains the USSR or Russia, which is on the verge of being definitively defeated. The enemy of the 21st century, which America created from scratch by welcoming it into the WTO, will be China, and France will continue to be an ally of the Americans, no matter what it does or says. Because France lost its independence in 1940.

text_en: You are deluding yourselves. The Russians had given up on striking western Ukraine, the bridges, or the railway nodes for months, and it’s clearly not out of leniency. It’s simply that their armaments do not allow them to effectively hinder Ukrainian military logistics, which adapted very well after the shock of the first days in February and can easily be supplied with heavy means (steel, cement, bridging equipment, generators, and fuel...) from the Polish border. The resumption of long-range strikes is solely because the Russians have decided to target civilian infrastructure, which is more vulnerable. But this blitz can only last a few weeks, and its effectiveness will decrease as the Ukrainians analyze the trajectories of the drones and receive modern air defenses. The Lend-Lease law has come into effect, and time is now on Ukraine's side.

text_en: Europeans, like the rest of the world, can start by not buying their production but their own. Linux is an operating system created by a European, the World Wide Web was created by a European, Nokia long ruled mobile phones, the Suse version of Linux has nothing to envy from Redhat. Without the missteps of the fabless company, Alcatel with Lucent would be a giant in telecoms. Between Soitec, the Italian-French ST MicroElectronics, and others, we can regain positions in electronics. What is missing above all is a real European sovereign fund to develop medium and long-term projects. We are not leaders in the software industry either (although SAP...) but we are not ridiculous either. I would feel more comfortable with the actions of the Gates Foundation if it had governance with disinterested personalities already known for their commitment. Not sure Musk measures all his responsibilities.

text_en: Several things to consider. First, the smaller and more decentralized an installation is, the lower its overall efficiency. Second, renewables consume more materials (concrete, metals, glass, etc.) per unit of energy produced than thermal power plants (fossil or nuclear), so more material needs to be extracted and transformed, which increases the energy consumed to manufacture them. Thus, for example, wind energy consumes 8000 tons of concrete per TWh compared to 760 tons for nuclear. This brings me to the last point to consider: the energy return rate of renewables is poor, even very poor. In some cases, the energy mobilized to build and maintain a photovoltaic solar park will systematically exceed the energy produced by that same park throughout its operational life. See in this sense the study titled "Energy Return on Energy Invested (ERoEI) for photovoltaic solar systems in regions of moderate insolation."

text_en: Erwin Schrödinger, in his book What is Life?, defines life as negentropy. Simple to understand: it captures energy (and resources) to maintain order. text_en: If you want to know the Russian soul, it is better to turn to writers. For example, "Barsuki" (The Badgers, 1924), by Leonid Leonov, which describes a peasant revolt against the communist order, which assigned a contested land for centuries to a neighboring village... We see, regardless of the nature of power, elemental forces of destruction emerging that nothing stops, in the face of a semblance of rational order... The former are always ready to manifest, whether from the top or bottom of society. There is indeed in the Russian soul this permanent conflict between the resurgence of a limitless violent instinct and the constructive will within the limits of simple reason. In my opinion, there is more understanding to be gained from this side than from that of a "masculinity" real or supposed, which takes things from a narrow perspective and explains nothing.

text_en: What is shocking about this article is that this war is the most documented war live. The battlefield can be seen from the sky. There are multiple free and easily accessible sources that talk about what is happening on the ground, both from the Ukrainian side and the Russian side, each obviously doing its own propaganda, aside from the fog of war. And we have... this "article". And the comments are in the same vein. Have you looked at other sources? Sources that talk about what is happening on the ground, day by day, with the number of missiles and bombs launched, the number of tanks bombed, the number of soldiers killed, wounded, captured, the precise movements in the areas, the ultra-rapid evolution of drones, etc.? Not those that comfort you in your opinion, all of them, from each side. And have you tried to cross-check all the information you have? Such an article and such comments are impossible after that.

This extends the current credits at their current level, without taking into account the massive envelope intended for Ukraine. Before questioning which war the West could win, it would be appropriate to look at which wars it has won... The Ukrainian conflict gave it the opportunity to implement a new concept: massive financial and material aid coupled with sanctions, without direct engagement on the ground. While aid is stumbling over budgetary constraints, sanctions are proving largely counterproductive: by tightening the oil tap, Moscow has managed to increase its revenues, despite the sanctions against it. Europe should take stock and ask itself if there is still a realistic hope of winning this war.

text_en: Electrons are not stored in warehouses like potatoes, beans, or wheat. If not consumed, they are not produced; I do not understand how this so-called electricity market works. In the era of famous AI, and even with real AI, it is entirely possible to predict future electricity consumption at the scale of states, at the hour, day, week, and even month if necessary, and to set up in advance the production capacity to meet it. Large local consumers can even make the effort to predict their own needs. The price of electricity is then the weighted average of its production cost at the national level. Surprising, right? At Total, the price at the pump depends on the price of the oil that supplied a particular station; everyone knows that! Is this electricity market not simply a market for speculators, with deceivers and the deceived?

text_en: Last night, there was a lot of mockery on TV about Macron and Leyden's "cuddle therapy" approach towards Xi, deeming it futile or even humiliating. It seems to me that this is not the case: it is the required politeness ceremony, especially when considering a very tight business negotiation. Xi does not want war because we are on the brink of social implosion in China: the long trend of expansion must continue. Europe (its state technological capitalism) is ready to step in where the USA withdraws. But a stalemate is possible, which if Russian pressure on Europe decreases, and Europe could potentially go against the USA (which, by the way, does not offer any tariff concessions to the EU). In reality, no discussion will address any of these topics, since it is the only tariff scale that China wants to discuss! The only truth of the subtext: these are the contracts and commercial commitments!

text_en: Mishustin was confirmed in his position not because he "faithfully executes" what Putin does not take care of, but because he has been very effective in conducting business, particularly in the economy under the hostility of NATO countries doing everything to sink it. The result was that Russia's GDP grew last year significantly more than that of EU countries, and this year it is accelerating even more. And contrary to the urban legend circulating in the media, the arms industry, with total military spending representing 6% of GDP, is not the driving force but is due to the increase in real incomes of the population that has driven consumption and the substitution of industrial products for those under embargo. text_en: "The press is free in Ukraine." Attention... another trick from the jokers without borders! This fairy tale still surprises the American organization Fairness and Accuracy in Reporting (FAIR). On December 29, Zelensky signed a new law that, under any other latitude, would have horrified RSF: The text indeed grants the National Council for Television and Radio Broadcasting (the Ukrainian media regulatory body) the power to fine, close, revoke licenses without trial, or block any title, channel, social media, or platform. However, the authorities closely control this council since Mr. Zelensky appoints four of the eight members and Parliament appoints four others. Let us bet that next year Ukraine will trail the United States, 45th in the RSF ranking. Let us remember that eleven political parties are still banned in Ukraine! All of them left-wing, as if by chance!

text_en: To the nine respondents above, draping oneself in the discourse of "he started it," "Ukraine is the victim," "international law says that..." is one thing: accessible to everyone. The problem is that we are not playing a game of chess or football against an opponent who is not fair-play or even a cheater. We are talking about a war involving more than 2 million soldiers over 1000 km of front and resulting in hundreds of thousands of dead and wounded. War is the ultimate balance of power; it is the strongest who wins, and the dead and maimed are real. Ending the conflict before Ukraine's complete defeat is not playing into the Russians' hands. It is saving what can be saved by negotiating a peace agreement that is undoubtedly imperfect but sufficiently balanced to get out of this deadly impasse. Unless, of course, the complete defeat of Ukraine is desired by the supporters of rearmament and a rematch in 5 or 10 years.

text_en: Two styles of obedience, understood as pre-political: submission and conformity. Submission is a form of obedience in which a powerful power relationship forces a will to bend under the yoke of another. Sometimes it is enough to simply believe that the other is strong. Conformity reveals something else: a horizontal alignment of wills. Being conformist is mainly about finding in obedience a refuge against loneliness, a bulwark against exposure. Following the general movement docilely to protect oneself because it is difficult to allow oneself to be oneself, to be different. In this case, as in the case of RN voters, it is the strongest conformity. And, let us remember (Asch's experiments in 1951): if more than 3 people make an absurd and false statement about a subject >> about 33% of subjects will be influenced and will make the same statement even if they objectively observe the opposite... CNews, FoxNews...

text_en: What he says seems sensible. Yes, it has been more than 10 years since the USA formalized the "pivot to Asia" (Obama, 2011). Yes, Putin cannot afford to lose (hence the fears of nuclear escalation) and today this risk is very low. The question is what will remain of Ukraine (in terms of territory, sovereignty, and defense capabilities). If Ukraine is truly folded, then Putin will have a seasoned army backed by a powerful war industry, a renewed confidence in himself, and accounts to settle in Europe: I would bet on a war (or a credible threat against concessions) even before the 2 years (as long as Trump has all powers). A frozen conflict, in which Ukraine retains the means to defend itself (and thus to attack if Russia were to launch its forces elsewhere) would be preferable for us... and thus probably unacceptable for Putin. The unknown is the degree of collusion between Trump and Putin.

title_en: The fate of Ukraine more precarious than ever after the spectacular confrontation between Donald Trump and Volodymyr Zelensky.

text_en: To limit the problem to our country, Thomas Piketty completely forgets that, as early as 1976, Prime Minister Raymond Barre declared: "France is living beyond its means." So, after half a century of uninterrupted budget deficits and 3300 billion in accumulated debt, how can we expect austerity in 2025 in a budget where expenses will exceed revenues by 40%? Unless one considers that a budget can be eternally in deficit, doesn't Mr. Piketty know that we can only start talking about austerity when a budget is in surplus? Moreover, isn't the norm for a budget to be in surplus?

title_en: Thomas Piketty: "Rather than austerity measures, Europe needs an investment plan."

text_en: Attacking justice, thus the law as Israel and the US do, especially in this case against the International Criminal Court, the highest criminal court in the world, is not only absurd, but it also necessarily demonstrates that one is not a democracy. Because respect for the law is an important pillar of any democratic state. Therefore, truly democratic states, especially in Western Europe, should take note and stop considering these two countries as democratic, let alone as sharing our values.

text_en: When you want to eat with the devil, you better have a long fork... Otherwise, he will eat us. Europe looks at the American giant like Mowgli looked at Kaa in the jungle book, fascinated, hypnotized. Europe, disunited, is ready to negotiate, in a submissive position. Europe no longer has a backbone; it slouches, bends under the blows of sovereignists and other national populisms that proudly show their muscles. Europe will become vassalized, vis-à-vis the USA, vis-à-vis Russia, vis-à-vis China. End of story. Hello dystopia.
